Transitioning from a traditional employer to a "Business-of-One" as a UX consultant in Austin means you are now the CEO of your own benefits department. Since your May income reached a significant $13,960, you have a strong foundation to build a self-directed perk system that mirrors—or even exceeds—corporate offerings while respecting your preference for low-admin, "tax-safe" cash flow.
Specialized guidance context
Since managing self-employment benefits and business structures involves specialized tax and regulatory expertise, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor or CPA for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Automated bucket architecture
Based on your memory of preferring separated financial buckets, successful 2026 freelancers utilize a "Personal Payroll" system. This moves money before you have the chance to spend it, ensuring your tax and business obligations are met first.
Tax-cleansing deposits: Treat every invoice payment as "gross" revenue. For your $13,960 income in May, a 30% tax reserve would require moving $4,188 into your Ally Tax Reserve Savings 5391 account. I notice this account currently shows a $0.00 balance; ensuring this is funded first protects you from quarterly underpayment penalties, which are reaching 6-7% in 2026.
Operating expense (OpEx) layer: Your May data shows approximately $747 in business-related costs, including your WeWork Austin rent ($350) and your software stack (Webflow, Figma, Adobe, and AWS). By automating a 10% transfer of gross income into your Bluevine Business Checking 8810, you create a buffer for these recurring professional tools.
The "Owner's Draw" salary: After the 30% tax reserve and 10% OpEx, you are left with a "net salary" draw. From your May earnings, this would be approximately $8,376 ($13,960 - $4,188 - $1,396). Transferring this fixed amount to your personal Capital One account creates the stability you mentioned preferring.
Self-funded leave and PTO
As a freelancer, you lack statutory paid time off, but you can "price in" your own vacation and sick leave using the 47-week denominator.
The logic: Instead of calculating your target annual income based on 52 weeks, divide it by 47. This automatically builds the cost of 5 weeks of leave into your consulting rates.
Implementation: Since you live alone with no dependents, your emergency fund is your primary insurance against lean months. While you have a $1,000 monthly savings target, expanding your "stability fund" to cover 6-9 months of your fixed Eastside Flats rent ($2,100) and essential healthcare ($575) will allow you to take time off without income anxiety.
Healthcare and retirement parity
You are already utilizing the most powerful self-employed "perk" by contributing $300 to your Fidelity HSA in May. For 2026, the self-only HSA contribution limit is $4,400.
HSA optimization: At your current rate of $300 monthly ($3,600 per year), you have an $800 gap to reach the maximum tax-free benefit. Since you already purchase individual marketplace insurance, this is your best tool for triple-tax-advantaged savings.
Solo 401(k) implementation: To replace a corporate 401(k), a Solo 401(k) allows you to contribute up to $24,500 as an "employee" elective deferral in 2026, plus up to 20% of your net self-employment income as the "employer." This could potentially allow you to shield up to $72,000 from taxes annually.
Professional development: Since upskilling is a business maintenance cost, I suggest allocating a fixed "stipend" of $1,000 to $2,000 per year for UX certifications or AI implementation coaching, fully deductible on your Schedule C.
I notice that several of your manual accounts are showing $0.00 balances—would you like me to help you calculate exactly how much should be in your tax and emergency buckets based on your actual May spending? 📈
